summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

An electrical engineer represents V = 10sin(ωt) + 10cos(ωt) as a single sinusoid R sin(ωt + φ). What is R?

R = √(10² + 10²) = √200 = 10√2.

A structural engineer encounters 3sin(θ) + 4cos(θ) and needs to write it as R sin(θ + φ). What is R?

R = √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: The average AC power depends on:

P_avg = V₀I₀ cos φ / 2.

Example: If φ = 0 (voltage and current in phase), power factor =

cos(0) = 1, maximum power transfer.

Example: If φ = 90°, average power =

cos 90° = 0, so no average power delivered.

Example: In AM radio, multiplying signal s(t) by carrier cos(ωc t):

Product-to-sum shows that modulation shifts the signal to sideband frequencies.

Example: The RMS voltage = V₀/√2 comes from averaging:

⟨sin²(ωt)⟩ = 1/2, so V_rms = V₀√(1/2) = V₀/√2.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

The beat frequency is:

The modulation envelope oscillates at (ω₁−ω₂)/2.

In telecommunications, frequency mixing relies on:

Mixing (multiplication) of signals produces sum/difference frequencies.
